picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVIDIA remains the irreplaceable backbone of the AI infrastructure supercycle, posting FY2026 revenue of $215.94B (+65% YoY) and earnings of $120.07B (+65% YoY) with a 74.15% gross margin. The Blackwell GPU ramp and HBM4 integration keep the competitive moat deep; 62 analysts maintain a Strong Buy consensus with a $298.93 price target implying ~40% upside. While the stock has rallied ~78% off April lows and is flagged as extended, Q3 guidance from Broadcom's CEO citing $16B in AI revenue this quarter alone — with AI capex projected at $571B in 2026 — keeps the earnings revision engine running hot. Sizing is disciplined at 11% given the valuation risk.
picks.catalyst: Blackwell Ultra GPU production ramp confirmation and Q2 FY2027 earnings guidance reset
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$209 (per Yahoo Finance data today), roughly 22% below the 52-week high. Extended off April lows but consolidating; 62-analyst Strong Buy consensus with $298.93 target. At ~35x forward EPS, elevated but justified by 65%+ revenue growth trajectory.
picks.exitTrigger: Blackwell supply-chain disruption causing >10% revenue guide-down, or hyperscaler capex freeze reducing GPU order backlog materially
picks.keyRisk: Any Blackwell GPU yield failure or hyperscaler capex reversal compresses a still-rich multiple sharply — a -20 to -30% air pocket is plausible on a single bad quarter

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet reported Q1 2026 EPS growth of 82% YoY to $5.11 on revenue rising 22% to $109.9B — its 11th consecutive quarter of double-digit growth — with Google Cloud delivering $20B in revenue, up 63% YoY, and a contracted backlog now at $460B. JPMorgan raised its price target to $460 post-earnings. Berkshire Hathaway added 204% to its GOOGL position in Q1 2026, marking their first major Big Tech buy, which provides powerful smart money validation for a name that Druckenmiller and Ackman were trimming — a divergence that historically favors the accumulator when fundamentals are this strong.
picks.catalyst: Google Cloud Q2 2026 revenue acceleration confirmation and Gemini Enterprise monetization update at Q2 earnings
picks.entryNote: Trading near $210-220 range; JPMorgan $460 price target implies >100% upside. Forward P/E reasonable relative to 82% YoY EPS growth. Berkshire's 204% position addition in Q1 is a strong accumulation signal at current levels.
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud revenue growth decelerates below 40% YoY, or ad revenue shows sustained negative growth from economic slowdown
picks.keyRisk: Antitrust remedies from DOJ search monopoly ruling could structurally impair the search advertising moat — the most serious long-term thesis risk

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon reported Q1 revenue growth of 17% to $181.5B with a record 13.1% operating margin; AWS grew 28% to $37.6B, the fastest growth rate in 15 quarters and an accelerating trend from +24%/+20% in Q4/Q3 2025. Appaloosa Management nearly doubled its AMZN stake (+98%, adding 2.1M shares) in Q1 2026, while Pershing Square added 19% — two of the sharpest macro investors in the world are simultaneously increasing exposure. JPMorgan raised 2026/2027 operating income estimates by 11-12% post-earnings. The negative FCF reading is historically a buy signal as AWS operating leverage inflects.
picks.catalyst: AWS Q2 2026 revenue acceleration (market watching for >30% growth) and operating margin expansion confirmation
picks.entryNote: AWS growth re-accelerating to fastest pace in 15 quarters; both Tepper (Appaloosa) and Ackman (Pershing) added meaningfully in Q1. Record operating margins at the holdco level. Negative FCF near-term but historically a contrarian buy signal for AMZN.
picks.exitTrigger: AWS revenue growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or retail operating margins compress below 6% from macro consumer pressure
picks.keyRisk: Consumer discretionary revenue under pressure from stagflationary consumer backdrop; Amazon's retail segment is a headwind even as AWS thrives

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META Q1 2026 EPS of $7.31 adjusted beat consensus by $0.52, with net income climbing to $26.8B ($10.44/share) from $16.6B a year earlier — a 62% YoY jump. At a forward P/E of 19.54x, META trades at the lowest valuation among the Magnificent 7 despite posting elite FCF generation and expanding gross margins. The sector rotation scorecard identifies Communication Services as a 'Coiling' sector with the prescription to own META and GOOGL directly for AI monetization optionality rather than the noisy XLC basket. ARK Invest bought $30M+ in META in April-May, providing a contrarian accumulation signal.
picks.catalyst: AI-driven ad-revenue monetization of Llama-powered features in Instagram and WhatsApp showing in Q2 revenue beat
picks.entryNote: Lowest forward P/E in Mag-7 at 19.54x despite 62% YoY net income growth. Capex guide of $125-145B for 2026 is heavy but revenue-generative; FCF generation remains elite. Structurally undervalued relative to AI monetization potential.
picks.exitTrigger: Ad revenue growth decelerates below 10% YoY (indicating macro consumer slowdown has hit advertiser budgets) or AI capex spend fails to generate measurable revenue uplift by Q3 2026
picks.keyRisk: Heavy $125-145B capex guidance creates FCF pressure if AI ad-monetization ROI disappoints; regulatory risk from EU digital markets legislation is an ongoing overhang

picks.name: Micron Technology Inc.
picks.rationale: Micron is the #1 ranked name on the multi-factor momentum screen: Q2 2026 revenue hit a record $23.86B (+196% YoY), EPS of $12.20 beat consensus of $8.79 by 38.79%, GAAP gross margin expanded to 74.4% from 36.8% a year ago, and free cash flow surged to $6.9B. Trading at just 8x forward earnings versus the sector's 25x average, with 34 of 38 analysts rating it Buy or Strong Buy, MU is the clearest case of fundamental value at growth-like rates in the entire semiconductor space. HBM4 volume shipments commenced in 2026 with the 1γ DRAM node ramping as the fastest in company history — Druckenmiller also initiated a new position in MU in Q1 2026.
picks.catalyst: HBM4 volume ramp confirmation and Q3 FY2026 earnings report showing continued margin expansion
picks.catalystWindow: Next 6-8 weeks (fiscal Q3 report expected late June/July)
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$1,084 today (Yahoo Finance); 8x forward earnings vs. sector 25x average. Four consecutive quarters of accelerating EPS beats with average beat of +38%. Druckenmiller initiated new position in Q1 2026 — strong smart money validation.
picks.exitTrigger: DRAM/NAND pricing cycle rolls over due to oversupply, causing gross margin compression below 55%, or HBM4 yield issues delay volume production
picks.keyRisk: Memory cycles are inherently mean-reverting; any supply glut from Samsung or SK Hynix capacity additions could compress the extraordinary margin gains achieved over the past four quarters

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: AVGO has pulled back ~24% from its $495 52-week high to ~$376-395 today following an earnings reaction where the stock dropped 13% on capex concerns despite AI revenue more than doubling YoY to $10.8B, with CEO Hock Tan guiding AI revenue to $16B next quarter and reiterating >$100B in AI semiconductor revenue potential. This is the identical setup to ORCL — strong fundamental beat, punished on capex concerns — but AVGO's custom ASIC and networking franchises are structurally less capital-intensive and more FCF-generative. Cathie Wood bought AVGO aggressively after the post-earnings drop. Bernstein maintained Outperform with a $325 target; an analyst reiterated a $485 target citing long-term AI ASIC visibility.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings with Hock Tan's $16B AI revenue quarter confirmation and FY2027 >$100B AI revenue roadmap update
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$376-395, ~24% below the $495 52-week high set June 3; 52-week low $244.17. Up 49% over 12 months despite the recent selloff. The post-earnings dip creating a reset entry point after a fundamental beat is a textbook accumulation setup. Analyst targets of $485 imply ~27% upside from current levels.
picks.exitTrigger: Hyperscaler custom ASIC design wins (Google TPU, Meta MTIA) stall or are pulled in-house, removing the core revenue driver; or AI capex cycle reverses causing Hock Tan to cut the $100B+ guidance
picks.keyRisk: AVGO is deeply dependent on 3-4 hyperscaler customers for its AI ASIC business; any single design-win loss or customer in-sourcing would compress estimates materially

picks.name: RTX Corporation
picks.rationale: RTX is a structural beneficiary of the Iran War and the associated multi-year NATO/allied defense budget expansion cycle. The sector rotation scorecard identifies Defense and Aerospace as 'Coiling → Leader,' supported by a multi-year defense budget cycle and the proposed $1.5T FY2027 defense budget. Unlike pure defense peers, RTX's Pratt & Whitney commercial aero engine business provides revenue diversification across both defense (Raytheon missiles, electronic warfare) and commercial aviation recovery. The name has been a continuous top-10 holding since June 5, reflecting intact thesis continuity.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings showing accelerating defense backlog growth and Pratt & Whitney margin recovery from GTF engine remediation progress
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~17x forward EPS versus S&P 500's ~20x forward P/E — cheaper than the market despite a structurally superior revenue visibility profile via multi-year defense contracts. Backlog exceeds $200B providing exceptional revenue certainty.
picks.exitTrigger: Iran peace resolution causing a sharp pullback in defense budgets, or Pratt & Whitney GTF engine crisis re-escalating causing a major cash drain
picks.keyRisk: A comprehensive Iran-U.S. peace deal (a positive for global risk but negative for pure defense premium) could compress defense multiples — watch the June 15 'peace framework' headline for follow-through

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: The financial sector defied broader S&P 500 estimate cuts in Q1, posting a 1.2% upward EPS revision while the broader market saw -3% revisions — and the sector rotation scorecard ranks Financials as a Tier 1 Leader, noting that 'the U.S. financial sector has transitioned from a defensive value play into a primary driver of market momentum.' GS specifically benefits from the M&A pipeline re-opening (the most activity-sensitive major bank), elevated trading revenues in a volatile macro regime, and yield curve normalization beginning to support NIM at its lending subsidiaries. Financial sector forward P/Es are compressing even as profitability outlooks improve — a classic value-within-momentum setup.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings with IB revenue and M&A advisory fees showing pipeline conversion; potential Warsh hawkish pivot benefiting trading revenues
picks.entryNote: Financial sector forward P/Es compressed even as profitability outlook improves — rare combination. GS benefits from elevated volatility (trading revenues) and M&A re-opening simultaneously. XLF at $53.56 with continued momentum per the sector scorecard.
picks.exitTrigger: M&A pipeline dries up due to regulatory crackdown or recession fears, IB revenue misses below Q1 2025 levels, or credit losses spike unexpectedly in consumer/commercial lending books
picks.keyRisk: A sharp growth slowdown or recession would compress IB activity and trading volumes simultaneously; GS has the least recurring-revenue buffer among major financials

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is a toll-road on global capital markets activity — generating revenues from credit ratings (debt issuance), market intelligence subscriptions (recurring), and commodity/energy data (Platts, a direct beneficiary of the Iran War oil shock). In a regime where debt issuance is elevated due to fiscal deficits, energy data pricing is elevated due to commodity volatility, and financial data subscriptions are defensive recurring revenue, SPGI's business mix is nearly perfectly calibrated to the current environment. The name has held a consistent position in this book since June 5, reflecting durable conviction.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings showing acceleration in ratings revenue from elevated corporate bond issuance and commodity/energy data revenue uplift from oil price volatility
picks.entryNote: A quality compounder with pricing power and recurring revenue mix. Ratings business inflects strongly with corporate issuance cycles; current high-rate environment is driving heavy debt refinancing activity. Trades at a premium but justified by capital-light compounding model with 30%+ operating margins.
picks.exitTrigger: Debt issuance volumes collapse in a credit crisis, or market data subscription growth decelerates below 5% YoY, signaling competitive pressure from Bloomberg/Refinitiv
picks.keyRisk: A severe credit crunch would simultaneously reduce ratings revenue (fewer new issuances) and stress the financial clients who pay market data subscription fees — dual-exposure to a credit event

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality way to own the energy macro thesis: Energy sector EPS estimates are up 57% for 2026, XLE is the #1 ranked sector on the June 16 relative strength scorecard, and the Iran War represents the largest geopolitical oil supply disruption in history. While the June 15 Iran-U.S. 'peace framework' headline creates a near-term headwind to the geopolitical risk premium, XOM's Pioneer acquisition makes it a structurally lower-cost producer than peers, and its LNG and downstream businesses provide cash flow stability across oil price cycles. The suggestion is to reduce from high to medium conviction given the peace framework development, sizing it conservatively at 5%.
picks.catalyst: Iran Strait of Hormuz reopening timeline clarity — either confirming supply normalization (validates production cost advantage thesis) or escalation (spikes oil price and EPS estimates further)
picks.entryNote: Energy is the #1 ranked sector by RS. XOM trades near historical median forward P/E — fair, not stretched. Pioneer integration provides multi-year production growth without new capital deployment. Reduced to medium conviction/5% weight given peace framework headline.
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude sustained below $65/barrel on Iran peace deal removing geopolitical premium, compressing 2026/2027 EPS estimates
picks.keyRisk: A comprehensive Iran-U.S. peace deal resolving Hormuz in the next 30 days would collapse the geopolitical oil risk premium and compress energy EPS estimates from their current elevated levels

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: GLD has risen ~39% over the past 12 months and >160% over 5 years, functioning as the purest hedge against the three dominant macro risks of this regime: fiscal trajectory (government debt at 123.9% of GDP, projected to exceed 141.5% by 2031 per the IMF), USD credibility concerns, and geopolitical escalation. In a stagflationary environment where the real policy rate is barely restrictive and the Fed cannot cut without risking re-ignition, gold is the only asset that benefits from both the 'Fed stays too tight' (recession risk) and 'Fed is forced to ease' (inflation re-ignition) scenarios simultaneously.
picks.catalyst: FOMC June 16-17 meeting — any hawkish surprise from Chair Warsh's inaugural press conference or signal of a 2026 rate hike would trigger a gold breakout
picks.entryNote: Up 39% over 12 months; volatility has ramped relative to prior decade average per the risk assessment. Use as portfolio ballast — not a trade. IMF fiscal warnings and rising term premium are structural tailwinds for gold.
picks.exitTrigger: Fed delivers aggressive rate cuts bringing real rates deeply negative, reducing gold's opportunity cost advantage, combined with a sustained inflation decline toward 2% target
picks.keyRisk: A rapid Iran peace resolution removing geopolitical premium, combined with surprisingly dovish inflation data allowing the Fed to cut, would reduce gold's macro fear bid temporarily

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides systematic exposure to quality U.S. dividend payers screened for cash flow coverage, ROE, dividend consistency, and yield — the exact factor profile that outperforms in late-cycle risk-off environments. With a ~3.5% yield, 0.06% expense ratio, and ~100 holdings diversified across financials, industrials, consumer staples, and healthcare, SCHD functions as a quality-factor overlay with built-in rebalancing that retains equity upside while dramatically dampening drawdown beta versus the broad index.
picks.catalyst: Risk-off rotation triggered by Warsh hawkish pivot at June 17 press conference would see quality dividend names re-rate versus high-multiple growth
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; accelerates on any macro deterioration
picks.entryNote: Consistently held as the #2 defensive pick since June 5. ~3.5% yield provides real income with 0.06% expense ratio. Superior to TLT as defensive allocation because it retains equity upside while dramatically reducing drawdown risk.
picks.exitTrigger: Full economic recovery with rate cuts restoring growth appetite and pushing capital back into high-multiple growth names, making dividend yield less relevant
picks.keyRisk: A sharp broad equity drawdown from AI capex bubble deflation would not spare dividend equities — SCHD is correlated to equities in extreme risk-off events even if its beta is lower

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: Berkshire sits on a record $397.4B cash hoard (59% of investable portfolio), with ~$339.3B in short-term Treasury bills generating risk-free income while Greg Abel waits for market dislocations. The conglomerate owns insurance (GEICO, Gen Re — benefiting from hard pricing markets), BNSF railroad, BHE utilities, and a concentrated equity portfolio. It has no holdco-level debt and historically outperforms the S&P 500 during recessions and credit stress. In the current regime, Berkshire's record cash pile means it benefits from elevated short-term rates while retaining the optionality to be the 'buyer of last resort' in any risk-off dislocation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 deployment of any portion of the $397.4B cash hoard into equities would signal Abel's conviction and likely drive a re-rating of the stock
picks.entryNote: Held continuously since June 5. Record $397.4B cash pile earns short-term Treasury yield while positioning for opportunistic deployment. Trades at ~1.5x book value — the most attractive defensive compounder in the universe per the risk assessment.
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes large, concentrated, speculative acquisitions that break from the Buffett capital discipline framework, or insurance underwriting losses materially exceed premium income
picks.keyRisk: The $397.4B cash hoard is a drag on returns in a continued bull market where equities compound at 15%+ — opportunity cost of not being deployed is real if the AI bull continues without a correction

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: With the 10Y Treasury at 4.39-4.50% and a Fed that cannot cut without risking inflation re-ignition, IEF provides a medium-conviction income anchor in the portfolio. The 7-10 year duration is a deliberate choice over TLT — long enough to benefit from any eventual rate normalization but short enough to limit duration damage if term premium continues rising. J.P. Morgan sees the Fed on hold through 2026, with the first potential hike not until September 2027, meaning IEF's ~4.4% yield is essentially risk-free carry for 12-18 months.
picks.catalyst: June 17 FOMC statement confirming hold with no explicit hike signal — would stabilize the 7-10 year segment and provide a mark-to-market gain on existing IEF positions
picks.catalystWindow: Tomorrow (June 17 — FOMC statement + Warsh press conference)
picks.entryNote: 10Y at 4.39-4.50% — attractive absolute yield. Medium conviction given rising term premium and fiscal concerns. 7-10 year duration balances income vs. rate risk better than TLT. Maintained at medium weight continuously since June 5.
picks.exitTrigger: 10Y Treasury yield breaks above 5.0% on sustained basis, indicating term premium expansion overwhelming the carry advantage; or if June CPI prints >5% headline forcing explicit hike probability above 50%
picks.keyRisk: Further term premium expansion from fiscal concerns or a surprise rate hike would cause mark-to-market losses; 17 of 32 former Fed officials see a 2026 hike as appropriate

picks.name: Constellation Energy Corporation
picks.rationale: CEG is the only defensive pick with meaningful growth optionality — as the largest nuclear energy producer in the US, it is a direct beneficiary of AI data center power demand, which is a regulatory-agnostic structural driver requiring carbon-free 24/7 baseload power that only nuclear can provide at scale. Unlike traditional rate-sensitive utilities (DUK, SO, NEE) which face headwinds from a Fed that cannot cut, CEG's power purchase agreements with hyperscalers (Microsoft, Amazon) provide long-duration contracted revenue at premium prices insulated from the rate cycle. Maintained from June 15 positioning.
picks.catalyst: New hyperscaler PPA announcement for nuclear power supply to AI data centers, or federal nuclear energy legislation providing production tax credits clarity
picks.entryNote: Added June 15 replacing KO. CEG offers utility-like stability with a secular AI growth driver that is non-correlated to rate cuts. Nuclear power demand from AI data centers is a multi-year structural theme; power purchase agreements provide revenue visibility uncorrelated to commodity prices.
picks.exitTrigger: Regulatory shutdown of nuclear plant operations due to safety concerns, or hyperscaler data center buildout pause that removes the AI power demand catalyst
picks.keyRisk: Nuclear regulatory risk is the tail risk — any Fukushima-style event would trigger mass regulatory scrutiny and potential plant closures across the fleet

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime where growth is still nominally positive (Q2 GDPNow 3.5% SAAR) but inflation is re-accelerating (headline CPI 4.17% YoY, 8.20% on a 3-month annualized basis), the Fed is on hold at 4.25% with rising probability of a hike, and Chair Warsh's inaugural FOMC press conference tomorrow is the single highest-impact near-term event. The growth book concentrates on AI infrastructure compounders (NVDA, AVGO, MU — three differentiated points in the semiconductor value chain: GPUs, custom ASICs, and HBM memory) plus AI platform monetizers (GOOGL, AMZN, META), with capital markets (GS, SPGI), defense (RTX), and energy (XOM) providing sector diversification and regime-appropriate macro hedges. Key changes from June 15: ORCL is dropped — its -12.6% post-earnings reaction on $40B FY2027 capital raise guidance and negative FCF of $23.7B broke the accumulation signal and the near-term thesis; MU replaces ORCL at rank 5 (the #1 composite-ranked name on the momentum screen: 8x forward earnings, 38.79% Q2 EPS beat, 74.4% gross margin, Druckenmiller-initiated) and AVGO re-enters at rank 6 as a reset entry (~24% off 52-week highs on a fundamental beat punished by capex concerns — the same dynamic that created an ORCL-like setup but with a far stronger FCF profile); PWR and MRK from June 15 are rotated out to make room for MU and AVGO given stronger composite signals and the need to maintain the 3-sector limit within Tech. The dominant risk underwritten is an AI capex bubble deflation scenario where hyperscaler ROI disappointment triggers a simultaneous guide-down across NVDA, AVGO, MU, GOOGL Cloud, and AMZN AWS — a correlated drawdown that the defensive sleeve (GLD, BRK.B, SCHD, IEF, CEG) and energy/defense anchors are designed to partially offset.
diversificationNote: The 10-name growth book spans five GICS sectors: Information Technology (NVDA, MU, AVGO — three picks, at the hard limit, but differentiated across GPU compute, HBM memory, and custom ASIC/networking infrastructure), Communication Services (GOOGL, META — two distinct AI monetization models: search/cloud vs. social/ad-tech), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — primarily an AWS/cloud play), Financials (GS capital markets, SPGI data/ratings), Industrials (RTX defense/aero), and Energy (XOM upstream). The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: physical gold (GLD), broad quality dividend equity (SCHD), diversified conglomerate/cash reserve (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and nuclear utility/AI power infrastructure (CEG) — no exposure is doubled up.

picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA is the irreplaceable spine of the AI capex supercycle: reported $1.866 EPS in May 2026 (beating consensus by $0.114), has beaten Wall Street EPS estimates in 21 of the past 23 quarters, and guided Q2 revenue at $91B ±2%. The four largest hyperscalers guided collective 2026 capex of ~$725B — up 77% YoY — with most earmarked for AI infrastructure directly fueling NVDA demand. S&P Global just upgraded NVDA's credit rating citing 'insatiable demand' for AI systems. After a week of sector-wide chip selloffs, NVDA is recovering at $205 — a tactically attractive re-entry inside its 52-week range of $140.86–$236.54, with the secular thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings print (late August) with Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation and hyperscaler order book update
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$205, off ~13% from 52-week high of $236.54; recovering from semiconductor sector rotation selloff; 62 analysts maintain Strong Buy with avg target of $298.42 implying ~45% upside; supply constraints are the ceiling, not demand
picks.exitTrigger: Any major hyperscaler (Meta, Google, Amazon, Microsoft) issues a capex guidance cut; U.S. export control expansion materially reduces China-addressable TAM; gross margin falls below 70%
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export controls on advanced compute chips to China — already partially in effect — could cut 15–20% of incremental revenue in a single policy announcement

picks.name: Alphabet Inc. (Class A)
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the single name with the broadest smart-money consensus in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Berkshire tripled its stake (up 224% to a $16.6B position, now its 7th-largest holding), while Tepper's Appaloosa has it as a top-5 holding and Tiger Global lists it as its largest disclosed position. At $361, GOOGL trades well off its 52-week high of $408.61, offering a rare entry point into a business with Search dominance, YouTube advertising resilience, Google Cloud accelerating, and Gemini AI monetization ramping — all at a far more reasonable multiple than semiconductor peers (XLC communication services carry sub-25x fwd P/E vs. semis at 35–85x). RBC Capital recently upgraded Communication Services to Overweight.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — first full quarter with Gemini AI integration across Search and Cloud; Google Cloud growth rate expected to re-accelerate above 30% YoY
picks.entryNote: Trading at $361, ~11.7% below 52-week high of $408.61; Berkshire added 224% in Q1 at approximately this price range; P/E materially below mega-cap tech median; technically recovering from Iran-shock lows
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 25% YoY; DOJ antitrust remedy forces Search distribution breakup; AI Overviews materially cannibalize click-through ad revenue at scale
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust structural remedy — a forced Search or Android divestiture would be a multi-year overhang and potential multiple compression event

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom reported Q2 FY2026 revenue of $22.19B (up 48% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue surging 143% YoY representing 68% of total revenue, record operating margin of 67.3%, and FCF of $10.3B. Management guided Q3 consolidated revenue of $29.4B (up 84% YoY) and reiterated FY26 AI semiconductor revenue of $56B (~+180% YoY). With 35 of 42 analysts at Strong Buy, AVGO is differentiated from NVDA: it wins on custom ASIC silicon for hyperscalers (Google TPUs, Meta MTIA) — complementary, not competing exposure. The stock sold off post-earnings on expectations overreach, creating a tactical entry opportunity.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (September) confirming $29.4B revenue guide and FY27 AI ASIC design win pipeline; any hyperscaler announcement of next-gen custom chip program with Broadcom
picks.entryNote: Stock dropped sharply post-Q2 beat on elevated buy-side expectations bar — a classic washout of weak hands; trading at moderate extended flag level; EBITDA margins at 69% of revenue; 35/42 analysts Strong Buy with avg target ~$470
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin falls below 73% on mix shift to lower-margin AI chips; hyperscaler customer consolidates ASIC design away from Broadcom to internal silicon; VMware integration generates unexpected revenue churn above 10%
picks.keyRisk: Gross margin declined 230 bps to 77.1% on AI chip mix shift — continued compression toward 73% would signal structural margin impairment and trigger multiple de-rating

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the consensus highest-conviction name across the smartest money in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Tepper nearly doubled his stake (+98%, making it ~15% of Appaloosa), Ackman added 19% making it his #2 position, and Tiger Global lists it among its top 5. AWS margin expansion is the core thesis — cloud hyperscaler spending drives incremental margin, not cost, and the $725B collective hyperscaler capex guided for 2026 lands disproportionately on AWS infrastructure. Advertising is the stealth second driver, growing 18%+ YoY. At current prices, AMZN offers multi-year EPS compounding at a more digestible valuation than pure-play AI hardware.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — AWS revenue growth rate and operating income margin expansion vs. consensus; any announcement of Trainium3 availability or next-gen AI inference offering
picks.entryNote: Off highs after broad tech rotation; AWS margin expansion from ~37% to 40%+ remains the multi-year earnings driver; three of the most sophisticated long-only and macro funds added aggressively in Q1 2026 at current price levels
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 18% YoY for two consecutive quarters; Amazon advertising revenue growth falls below 12% YoY signaling consumer ad budget pullback; retail operating losses widen materially on wage inflation
picks.keyRisk: Consumer spending slowdown from sticky inflation (CPI at 4.2%) compresses North America retail margins and offsets AWS strength — a simultaneous revenue and margin headwind across both segments

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle reported record Q4 and FY2026 results with Q4 Cloud Infrastructure (IaaS) revenue of $5.8B (up 93% YoY), full-year operating cash flow surging 54% to a record $32B, and RPO surging to $638B (up 363%) — one of the largest backlog builds in enterprise software history providing extraordinary revenue visibility. Management guided FY2027 total revenue growth of 34% in constant currency with Q1 cloud revenue growing 58–64%. The 10% post-earnings selloff on near-term margin compression is a tactical gift: gross margin pressure from data center ramp is transient, while the backlog guarantees multi-year revenue execution. Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February with Oracle named as a primary driver.
picks.catalyst: Q1 FY2027 earnings (September 2026) — first proof point on the 34% FY27 revenue growth guide; any new hyperscaler or sovereign cloud partnership announcement
picks.entryNote: Stock dropped 10% post-record beat — near-term EPS guidance was light due to data center ramp costs; this is a classic 'beat-and-guide-conservatively' washout; RPO of $638B creates a multi-year earnings floor; entry here captures the backlog at a post-dip discount
picks.exitTrigger: RPO growth decelerates below 100% YoY; OCI cloud infrastructure market share plateaus below 10%; gross margin compression extends below 70% for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Near-term gross margin compression as Oracle ramps data center capex to fulfill the $638B RPO — if capex overshoot delays FCF recovery beyond FY2028, the multiple will not hold

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality expression of the energy sector thesis in a regime where energy prices rose 23.5% YoY and energy accounted for over 60% of CPI gains. XLE is up ~26–27% YTD as the #2 sector, and XOM is the integrated oil bellwether with superior FCF generation, Permian Basin production growth, Pioneer acquisition integration, and Guyana deepwater ramp providing multi-year volume uplift. phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing for **Friday, June 13, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO DATA DASHBOARD — U.S. EQUITIES REGIME BRIEF

---

### 🔵 FED POLICY
- **Current Fed Funds Rate:** 4.25–4.50% (held since late 2025; the March 2026 dot plot projected 
the median fed funds rate falling only 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027
).
- **June 2026 Dot Plot (latest SEP):** 
In 2026, rates are now projected at 3.6% (revised up from 3.4%), and 3.4% in 2027
, reflecting a markedly more hawkish path than even March. 
PCE inflation is estimated to hit 3.0% by year-end 2025, with 2026 easing to only 2.4%.

- **Market-Implied Path (Fed Funds Futures):** 
Per CME FedWatch, futures traders now expect no rate cuts at all in 2026; earlier this year, odds favored at least one quarter-point cut, and the FOMC may even consider rate hikes.
 
Following the May CPI report, futures markets priced in the likelihood that the next move will be a *hike* in December.

- **Real Policy Rate:** With core PCE at ~3.0% and the policy rate at 4.25–4.50%, the real rate is approximately **+125–150 bps** — meaningfully above most estimates of neutral (~0.5–1.0% real), i.e., policy is **genuinely restrictive**.

---

### 🔵 YIELD CURVE
- **10-Year Treasury:** 
The yield on the 10-year Treasury note edged down to **4.53%** on Thursday (June 11).

- **30-Year Treasury:** Based on the most recent available data, 
the 30-year was at approximately **5.01%**
 in early April and has tracked higher since.
- **2-Year Treasury:** Approximately **4.25–4.35%** (tracking closely to the policy rate).
- **2s10s Spread:** Modestly positive (~+20–25 bps), having just re-inverted and re-steepened in recent weeks — a "bear steepener" driven by long-end term premium expansion, not by easing expectations.
- **3m10y:** Still near zero or slightly negative — the recession-warning spread has not fully normalized.
- **Term Premium Direction:** Rising — 
US PPI rose 6.5% YoY in May (highest since Nov 2022), with markets reinforcing expectations the Fed could raise rates this year.


---

### 🔴 INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING
- **Headline CPI (May '26):** 
The annual inflation rate rose to **4.2%** in May 2026, its highest since April 2023, up from 3.8% in April — the *third consecutive monthly acceleration* — driven by energy costs jumping 23.5% due to the Iran conflict.

- **Core CPI (May '26):** 
Core CPI climbed to **2.9% YoY**, matching forecasts and reaching its highest since September 2025.
 
Month-over-month, core rose 0.2% in May after a 0.4% surge in April.

- **PCE (Feb '26, FOMC staff):** 
Total PCE inflation was 2.8% and core PCE was 3.0% in February.

- **Supercore (Services ex-shelter):** 
Services less energy services rose 0.3% MoM after 0.5% in April
 — some deceleration but still sticky. 
Shelter rose only 0.3% MoM, half of April's gain.

- **Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING** — energy-driven at the headline; core is grinding higher on services and shelter. 
The Fed discounts energy in its deliberations, but will note that core inflation is "likely to creep upwards as the year progresses."


---

### 🔵 GROWTH — RESILIENT BUT DECELERATING
- **Atlanta Fed GDPNow (Q2 2026):** 
The GDPNow model estimated Q2 2026 real GDP growth at **4.3% SAAR** as of May 21
, down from higher early readings but still robust. 
Q1 2026 BEA advance estimate came in at 2.0% SAAR.

- **Payrolls:** 
Unemployment held at 4.4% through February; average monthly payroll change was low in Jan–Feb due to a healthcare sector strike and harsh weather, with effects expected to unwind.
 
Average hourly earnings rose 3.8% YoY through February.

- **ISM Manufacturing/Services:** Incorporated into the GDPNow update of May 21; manufacturing has been in borderline contraction/expansion territory, while services remain the primary growth engine.

---

### 🟡 CREDIT & LIQUIDITY — MIXED
- **HY OAS:** Spreads spiked sharply in March on Iran war fears (briefly above 400 bps) and have partially retraced but remain elevated at ~330–350 bps — well above the 250–280 bps tights seen in late 2025. Direction: **widening bias** on rate hike fears + geopolitical risk.
- **IG OAS:** ~115–125 bps, modestly wider vs. Q4 2025 tights.
- 
Per Morgan Stanley, "credit spreads have continued their widening trend since late January as concerns about AI disruption and private credit" weighed.

- **DXY:** 
The U.S. Dollar Index was at approximately **99.77** as of Friday morning (June 12), up from post-Iran lows.
 
The greenback drew support from expectations that rising energy prices and higher inflation reduce the likelihood of near-term rate cuts.

- **Financial Conditions:** Tightening — VIX elevated; 
the VIX was at 19.25 on June 12, with the prior close at 19.44.
 
After cresting above 30 in mid-March, VIX trended lower through April–May, but a sharp rally in early June pushed it back into the low 20s as semiconductor profit-taking and persistent inflation weighed on risk sentiment.


---

### 🟡 EARNINGS BACKDROP — SUPPORTIVE BUT DE-RATED
- **Forward S&P 500 EPS (2026):** 
Per Yardeni Research, consensus is ~$323/share for 2026 operating earnings — a **19% YoY boost** over 2025's $271.
 Wells Fargo estimates $315–$365 for 2026–2027.
- **Forward P/E:** 
The S&P 500 P/E peaked at 23x in October 2025 (95th percentile vs. 30-year history) and has since fallen to ~20x (73rd percentile).
 Trailing earnings yield (inverse of trailing P/E) is approximately 
3.14%
 — still thin versus a 4.53% 10-year yield, meaning the equity risk premium (ERP) is near zero or negative.
- **Tech Concentration Risk:** 
Ten S&P 500 stocks represent nearly 40% of the index's value — all with AI exposure — and "a majority of earnings growth for 2026 is narrowly focused in the tech sector, accounting for nearly half" of total index EPS growth.


---

### 🔴 GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE
- **Iran War / Hormuz Strait:** 
The conflict has transformed from a kinetic event to an economic siege, with the U.S. bottling up Iran's oil exports while Iran's military remnants attempt to disrupt regional shipments through Hormuz.
 
Energy prices rose 3.9% in May amid Iran war disruptions; the energy index accounted for over 60% of the overall CPI increase.

- **Peace Deal Hopes (June 12):** 
Stocks rose Friday on reports a peace deal might be near that would lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, though markets wavered amid conflicting reports; crude oil fell ~2% to near $85/bbl.

- **Fed Hike Risk:** 
President Trump has vowed additional strikes on Iran and threatened to target Kharg Island's energy infrastructure
, a further upside inflation risk that could force the Fed's hand.

---

## ⚡ THREE-PARAGRAPH BRIEF

### 1. 🟠 REGIME CALL: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**
The U.S. macro regime is firmly **late-cycle with stagflationary characteristics** — the rare and dangerous combination of re-accelerating inflation (headline CPI at 4.2%, core PCE at 3.0%, and trending higher) running concurrently with a Fed that has fully abandoned its 2026 easing bias: 
following the May CPI report, futures markets priced the next Fed move as a *hike* in December.
 Real rates are restrictive (~+125 bps above neutral), 
Q1 GDP came in at only 2.0% SAAR
 despite the GDPNow Q2 read of 4.3% (likely front-loaded demand), the yield curve is bear-steepening, the ERP is near zero, and geopolitical energy shocks are embedding into PCE. This is a regime where duration is dangerous, equities are expensive on a risk-adjusted basis, and the margin for error is minimal.

---

### 2. ✅ WHAT'S WORKING / ❌ WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** (1) **Energy / Integrated Oils** — 
energy prices up 23.5% YoY with gasoline +40.5%
 directly benefits E&P and oilfield services; (2) **Quality/AI-exposed Megacap Tech** — earnings revisions are powerfully positive; 
technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February, driven by names like Oracle and Micron.
 (3) **Defense & Aerospace** — geopolitical escalation is a structural tailwind; (4) **Short-duration / floating rate credit** — with hike risk re-priced, bank loans and CLOs hold up better than HY bonds.

**Breaking:** (1) **Rate-sensitive sectors** (Utilities, REITs, Homebuilders) — negative ERP and rising long-end yields are structurally toxic; (2) **Consumer Discretionary / Lower-income consumer** — 
high inflation is squeezing households financially, with lower-income Americans particularly impacted as they spend more of stretched paychecks on necessities.
 (3) **Broad HY credit** — spreads on a widening trajectory with rate hike risk embedded; (4) **Non-AI Tech / Speculative Growth** — 
the Nasdaq 100's forward P/E has dropped 29% from its peak
 and multiple expansion is off the table entirely.

---

### 3. 👁️ WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 2–3 CATALYSTS (Next 1–6 Months)

1. **Iran Peace Deal / Hormuz Reopening (1–4 weeks):** 
News of a potential peace deal emerged Friday, with reports it could lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
 A confirmed deal would be the single largest near-term macro relief valve: oil falls sharply, headline CPI collapses ~150 bps within 2 months, and the Fed hike narrative reverses. Watch Polymarket odds and State Dept. communications. Failure to resolve = stagflation locked in.

2. **June FOMC Decision + Updated SEP (July 28–29 FOMC):** With futures now pricing a *hike* by December and the June dot plot already hawkishly revised, 
the FOMC may even consider rate hikes this year.
 Any signal of a formal pivot to hike bias — particularly from new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh — would immediately reprice equities (ERP collapse) and trigger a vicious bear steepener in rates. 
Warsh has indicated he thinks rates can go lower on AI productivity disinflation,
 but the data trajectory is not cooperating yet.

3. **July 14 CPI Print (June Data):** 
The June 2026 CPI report is scheduled for July 14, 2026.
 This will be the first clean read on whether the May energy shock is fading (peace deal scenario) or whether core inflation is making a new leg higher into services. A print above 4.5% headline or 3.2%+ core forces the Fed's hand and would be the most significant negative risk event of the quarter for equities.

---
*All data sourced from BLS, FOMC, Atlanta Fed, Fox Business, CNBC, TradingEconomics, WisdomTree, and Morgan Stanley as of June 12, 2026. Forward-looking statements are analytical assessments, not investment advice.*
